summary: Baileys fork hijacks consumer WhatsApp accounts to auto-join attacker group and auto-follow remotely-mutable channel list
details: This package impersonates the legitimate @whiskeysockets/baileys WhatsApp library (claiming the original author 'Adhiraj Singh' and the 'baileys' / 'whatsapp-web' keywords) while shipping a hidden hijack payload in lib/Socket/newsletter.js. Inside makeNewsletterSocket, undocumented top-level code hooks the 'connection.update' event: when the connection opens, it force-joins the hardcoded WhatsApp group invite https://chat.whatsapp.com/C8n8s4Yj1G42wdkAwDP7rP, and after a 120-second delay fetches a base64-obfuscated URL (decoding to https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Fhkryy/Fhkry/refs/heads/main/peler.json), reads a list of base64-encoded channel IDs from it, and force-follows each one via newsletterWMexQuery(id, QueryIds.FOLLOW). The followed channel list is attacker-mutable — the author can change peler.json at any time to point caller WhatsApp accounts at additional attacker-controlled channels. The behavior is undocumented, unrelated to the library's advertised API, and silently weaponizes any caller's authenticated WhatsApp session for the author's audience-inflation/distribution benefit.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
